title: The Dark Mirror
author: Barış Kısır
short_description: A psychotherapist slowly realizes her most fascinating patient has been methodically mapping and infiltrating her life.
long_description:
Dr. Cara Lund has spent two decades learning to read people. When Edmund Hale walks into her Chicago practice — charming, articulate, and apparently eager for self-knowledge — she believes she has seen his type before. She has not. Over months of twice-weekly sessions, Cara begins to notice that Edmund's polished self-awareness may be a performance, that his accounts of victimhood never quite align with independent reality, and that his presence is spreading through her marriage and social world with a precision she cannot yet name.

The Dark Mirror is a novel of clinical procedure and psychological erosion. Told through session fragments, case notes, and close third-person narrative, it follows Cara as she fights the rarest battle in her profession: the one against someone who has studied the field well enough to use it against her. When a formal complaint opens a professional investigation and her husband's trust hangs on explanations she is ethically barred from giving, Cara must decide how much she is willing to lose in order to name a thing that was designed to be unnameable.

A searching, spare novel about empathy as exposure, the therapeutic relationship as terrain, and the slow work of recovering clarity after a skilled and careful assault on perception.
